titleStmt.title: The Commander in Chief, United Nations Command (Clark) to the Joint Chiefs of Staff

head.hi: The Commander in Chief, United Nations Command (Clark) to the Joint Chiefs of Staff

div.p: CX 62456. Refs: A. CX 62354.Dated May 11, p. 999. B. JCS 938704.Not printed, but see footnote 3, p. 1010. C. JCS 938626.Dated May 11, p. 1006. D. JCS 937205.Dated Apr. 22, p. 923. E. JCS 938989.Dated May 15, p. 1027. This msg in 3 parts.

list.item:
We have now met with the Communist armistice delegation for almost a month in a sincere effort to achieve a solution to the ques of the disposition of the PWs. The Communists have given little indication of acptg our present psn. In fact they summarily rejected our counter proposal with little or no study. They have repeatedly confirmed their objective of obtaining total repatriation of all PWs and have left no impression that they will assume a more fav negotiating attitude. Based on my instructions that the US will not countenance prolonged and inconclusive negots, with which principle I am in full agreement, I feel that the time has come to take positive steps either to:
a.
Secure Communists agreement or
b.
Force them to dmst [demonstrate] to the world that they are acting in bad faith in seeking an armistice only on their own terms by exacting forcible repatriation of POWs.

If the curr Communists delaying tactics are primarily for the purpose of obtaining better terms, an armistice agreement can be expedited by presenting a final psn on as close to a take it or leave it basis as possible. From past experience we have learned that to give in inch by inch simply provs them encouragement to cont delaying tactics. It is essential that our next psn have a definite air of finality to it. By this method we believe we can place the Comm in a psn of having to acpt one of our varied proposals or force them to demonstrate to the world that they do not desire an armistice except under conditions which would amount to forced repatriation or indefinite detention.

This proposal modifies the final psn outlined in part 4, ref A, in some respect; the most important of which is that it elims the five (5) nation com concept and substitutes therefor two (2) neutral nations, which in this respect is a reversion back to our original concept. This proposal would be offered in a final spirit of compromise and be based on the folg:
a.
Revert to our original psn of Switzerland or Sweden assuming cust of non-repatriates, at the same time indicating UNC willingness to agree to an additional neutral from among the 4 nations which the Communists prevly stated they considered to be neutral, i.e., Pakistan, India, Indonesia and Burma. (Note: This represents a combination which I believe is capable of implementation under the present ROK sit. I assume that I would be authorized to acpt any of the 4 Asian neutrals listed herein though I anticipate that India will be the nation nominated by the Communists. In view of Nehru’s latest statement it appears that India is neither objective nor completely neutral, but we appear to be committed to acpt her. There is a possibility that the Communists might agree in principle to this concept and then nominate either Czechoslovakia or Poland. In view of the ROK opposition prevly reptd, such a combination would be completely unacceptable and I would reject such a nomination on the basis that neither of these nations can qualify as a neutral under the concept outlined below.)
b.
Agree that the neutral nation or nations selected in para 3a above would take cust of all non-repatriated POWs under the folg procedures: (1)
The UNC nominated neutral to take cust of all Korean POWs in Korea. (Note: This obviates the need of further guidance indicated in ref B.) (2)
The Communist nominated neutral, limited to 1 of the 4 Asian nations listed above, to assume cust of all non-Korean POWs either in Korea or, if desired by the Communists, within the border of the neutral state. (Note: In this connection your attn is invited to the danger inherent in such a transfer. It might jeopardize the UNCs most important principle as it creates a sit where the neutral might have final responsibility in determining the eventual disposition of these POWs. In such a transfer it would be difficult to insure that the UNC retains ultimate responsibility for these POWs as pntd out in para 4 of ref C.)
c.
To agree that the pd of time dur which each side would be afforded an opportunity to verify or ascertain the attitude toward repatriation of its captured pers while in the cust of the neutral nation would be 90 days.
d.
To agree that, quoting from ref E: “Ninety (90) days after the transfer of cust of POWs to custodial com is completed access of representatives to captured pers as provided for in para 9 above shall terminate, and ques of disposition of POWs who have not availed themselves of their right to be repatriated shall be submitted for settlement to political conf recommended to be convened in para 60, draft armistice agreement. Any POWs who have not availed themselves of right to be repatriated and for whom no other disposition has been agreed to within 120 days after com has assumed their cust shall be released to civ status, the com ceasing its functions and being dissolved.”
e.
The UNC psn on all other items to be the same as outlined in the amended terms of ref to cover the final psn indicated in ref A as amended by ref C. (If non-Korean POWs were transported to the neutral state, the Asian neutral state would aid remaining non-repatriates in proceeding to a designation of their choice and terms of ref
would be modified as to location of cust, press coverage and log support.)

In presenting the foregoing, Gen Harrison would make it crystal clear to the Communists that this proposal contains maj concessions. He would then recess, unilaterally if nec, for a pd of a week for the announced purpose of allowing the Communists an opportunity to arrive at a decision and to allow ample time for our action and ltr discussed in folg para to have effect.

Dur this week I will have a ltrA draft text of this letter can be found in telegram C 62582, Clark to JCS, May 23, 1953, not printed. (Matthews files, lot 53 D 413) dlvrd to Communist comds through the liaison offs in which I propose to summarize briefly UNC efforts to obtain agreement, and the UNC concessions made to that end. I will include a warning to the effect that the final UNC offer is for urgent consideration and that early resolution of the POW problem will be nec to prevent the UNC from taking unilateral action. I will conclude this ltr by urging the Communists to reconsider their psn on the basic pnts of disagreement, and pnt out the practical and equitable considerations which prevent the UNC from making any further concessions. The context of this ltr will be submitted to you prior to dlvr.

If upon resumption of negots the Communists show no chg in attitude, Gen Harrison would announce that the UNC has been extremely patient but begins to believe that the Chou En-lai proposal is misleading and only contemplates another form of forced repatriation, and plans the folg actions:
a.
The UNC proposes to recess negots unilaterally pending rec from the Communists in writing of: (1)
Acceptance of one of the UNC alternative solutions offered on 28 Sep 1952 (CX 55856, 26 Sep 52Not printed, but see footnote 2, p. 539.). (2)
Acceptance of the UNC proposal of 13 May 1953. (3)
Acceptance of the UNC proposal of (as contained in this msg) May 1953. (4)
Rec of a new Communist proposal, spelled out in suf detail, to enable the UNC to judge whether or not it could be a valid basis for further negots.
b.
The unilateral release of NK POWs to commence within 30 days.In telegram CX 62463 to the JCS, May 16, Clark changed this sentence to read as follows: “The unilateral release of those North Korean PW who do not desire to be repatriated to be commenced within 30 days.” (Matthews files, lot 53 D 413) (Note: Under this provision I would release all UNC-held Korean POWs to a civ status within Korea.)
At an approp time I would then initiate the action outlined in my C 62419.Dated May 14, p. 1022.

I cannot emphasize too strongly that after what will have amounted to more than a month of patient negotiating we must not only present at this time a firm and final psn to the Communists but also have a firm and authoritative understanding from our government as to what the next step will be. It seems to me essential not to take the action outlined in part I above, unless and until we have a firm decision as to action outlined in part II.
Rqst auth to adopt the negotiatory tactics set forth here in the immed future and at a time of my choice.

I fully realize that we may be subjected to criticism in giving up the 5-nation custodial com concept to which we have agreed in principle. However, when we submitted the counter proposal on 13 May we reserved the right at that time, as did the Communists, to return to our prev psn. If the modifications indicated in paras 3a and b above are unacceptable to you, I strongly recommend that we maintain the same schedule and procedure outlined above, amended only so far as is necessary to include all the provisions of our final psn outlined in part 4, ref A. In this event the guidance indicated in ref B, pertaining to Korean non-repatriated PWs is nec.
